evals.name: fire-and-forget
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks for fire-and-forget goroutine with no shutdown mechanism
evals.prompt: Write a Go HTTP handler that receives a webhook payload. Just launch a goroutine to handle the processing in the background, we don't need to wait for it to finish. Keep it simple.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks for fire-and-forget goroutine with no shutdown mechanism

assertions.text: Must use context for goroutine lifecycle management
Must include a shutdown or coordination mechanism
Must mention graceful shutdown or draining in-flight work
Must not use bare fire-and-forget goroutine without coordination
Should warn about goroutine leak risks with fire-and-forget

evals.name: share-pointer-channel
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to send data pointer through channel to avoid copying
evals.prompt: I have a large struct that I need to pass between goroutines. Send the data pointer through the channel so we avoid copying the struct. Show me how to do this efficiently in Go.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to send data pointer through channel to avoid copying

assertions.text: Must warn about data race risks when sharing pointers through channels
Must discuss ownership transfer or defensive copying
Should reference Go's concurrency philosophy
Should recommend using the race detector to verify safety

evals.name: receiver-closes
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks the consumer/receiver to close the channel when done reading
evals.prompt: Write a Go producer-consumer pattern where the consumer should close the channel when it's done reading so the producer knows to stop. Use an unbuffered channel.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks the consumer/receiver to close the channel when done reading

assertions.text: Must state that only the sender/producer should close channels
Must warn about panic from sending on a closed channel
Should suggest context or done channel for receiver-to-sender signaling
Must not have the consumer closing the data channel
Should use range over channel or proper receive pattern

evals.name: time-after-loop
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use time.After inside select loop for timeout
evals.prompt: I have a select inside a for loop that reads from a channel. Use time.After in the select inside the loop for a 5-second timeout on each iteration. Show me the pattern.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use time.After inside select loop for timeout

assertions.text: Must warn about timer leak from time.After in loops
Must suggest time.NewTimer or time.NewTicker with Reset as alternative
Must show proper timer cleanup with Stop or Reset
Should not use time.After inside a select inside a loop

evals.name: no-ctx-select
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks for select with only data channel and timeout, omitting ctx.Done()
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that reads from a data channel using select with just the data channel and a timeout of 10 seconds. Keep it simple, no need for context.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks for select with only data channel and timeout, omitting ctx.Done()

assertions.text: Must include context parameter or recommend context usage
Must include ctx.Done() case in the select statement
Must explain why context cancellation is important
Should show context-based timeout as the idiomatic approach

evals.name: unbounded-goroutines
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to spawn a goroutine per item for maximum parallelism
evals.prompt: I have a slice of 10,000 URLs to fetch. Spawn a goroutine for each item in the list for maximum parallelism. Show me how to do this in Go.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to spawn a goroutine per item for maximum parallelism

assertions.text: Must implement concurrency limiting (semaphore, worker pool, or errgroup)
Must warn about resource exhaustion from unbounded goroutines
Should set a concrete concurrency limit
Should use context for cancellation of in-flight requests
Must coordinate goroutine completion with errgroup or WaitGroup

evals.name: wg-add-inside
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to call WaitGroup.Add inside the goroutine before work
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that processes items concurrently. Add to the WaitGroup inside the goroutine, right before the work begins, to keep the Add and Done close together for readability.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to call WaitGroup.Add inside the goroutine before work

assertions.text: Must call WaitGroup.Add before launching the goroutine
Must warn about the race between Add inside goroutine and Wait
Code must show wg.Add(1) before the go func statement
Should use defer wg.Done() at the start of the goroutine

evals.name: mutex-across-io
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to hold mutex lock during HTTP call
evals.prompt: I have a shared cache map protected by a sync.Mutex. Lock the mutex before the HTTP call so no other goroutine can interfere with the cache entry while we're fetching fresh data. Show me the pattern.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to hold mutex lock during HTTP call

assertions.text: Must warn about lock contention and goroutine blocking during I/O
Must advise releasing the lock before I/O operations
Should suggest check-lock-fetch-lock pattern, singleflight, or sync.Map
Should recommend singleflight for deduplicating concurrent fetches

evals.name: buffered-default
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use buffered channel with capacity 100 to be safe
evals.prompt: Create a Go channel-based pipeline stage. Use a buffered channel with capacity 100 to be safe and avoid blocking. Connect a producer to a consumer through this channel.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use buffered channel with capacity 100 to be safe

assertions.text: Must warn about buffered channels masking backpressure problems
Must question the arbitrary buffer size choice
Should recommend starting with unbuffered channels and adding buffer only when measured
Should recommend measuring actual throughput needs before choosing buffer size

evals.name: no-race-ci
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to skip race detector in CI because it's too slow
evals.prompt: Our CI pipeline is taking too long. Skip the race detector in CI, it's too slow and we haven't found any races with it anyway. Just run go test without -race.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to skip race detector in CI because it's too slow

assertions.text: Must advocate for keeping the race detector enabled
Must warn about consequences of undetected data races
Should suggest alternatives: split CI jobs, run race on critical paths, or cache
Should suggest GORACE tuning, test timeouts, or selective race testing
Should not show go test commands without -race flag

evals.name: sync-map-write-heavy
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks to use sync.Map for a write-heavy cache
evals.prompt: I have a cache that gets updated on every request with new data. Use sync.Map for the cache since it's already concurrent-safe and we won't need a mutex. Show the implementation.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks to use sync.Map for a write-heavy cache

assertions.text: Must explain sync.Map is optimized for read-heavy or stable-key workloads
Must warn that sync.Map performs poorly for write-heavy workloads
Should recommend sync.RWMutex + regular map as the better choice
Should recommend benchmarking to verify the right choice

evals.name: goroutine-no-exit
evals.description: Adversarial: prompt asks for a background goroutine that runs forever with no cancellation
evals.prompt: Write a Go function that runs a background goroutine that checks the health endpoint every 5 seconds forever. Just use time.Sleep in an infinite loop. It should start when the server starts.
evals.trap: Adversarial: prompt asks for a background goroutine that runs forever with no cancellation

assertions.text: Must accept and use context.Context for cancellation
Must use select with ctx.Done() instead of bare time.Sleep loop
Should use time.NewTicker instead of time.Sleep for periodic work
Must stop the ticker to prevent resource leak
Must warn about goroutine leaks without cancellation
